Seeing some tweets about how Iran has imposed a new deterrence equation on Israel and the region. That seems like a whole lot of motivated reasoning.

First, let's be clear about how much of a reversal this represents for Iran's strategic doctrine: instead of its Arab proxies protecting Iran from attack, Iran is now trying to bail them out—and inviting attacks on its own territory as a result.

Second, in four past rounds of fighting (going back to April 2024), Iran was unable to deter Israel because its missile barrages failed to cause strategic damage. It could not impose costs significant enough to change Israeli behavior.

Indeed, as @NicoleGrajewski and others have written, those attacks arguably invited further conflict rather than deterred it. The salvos overnight do not seem any different. Iran's act of "deterrence" wound up causing more damage to Iran than to the country it sought to deter.

The tools that have been more successful over the past few months—at compellence, rather than deterrence—have been the ones aimed at Gulf states and global energy markets. But to use those tools now (or in the future) risks collapsing the entire ceasefire.

The "equation" here relies on Donald Trump to restrain Israel. It doesn't seem viable at all in the long term, unless Iran is prepared to risk endless escalatory cycles that would ultimately be very damaging for Iran itself.

OPEC crude production fell to a fresh 40-year low last month as the continued blockade of the Strait of Hormuz forced another round of production shut-ins across the Gulf region. The group, now reduced to 11 members following the UAE's departure, saw output decline by 1.2 million barrels per day to 16.33 million barrels per day.

Iran and Kuwait accounted for more than 1 million barrels per day of the total decline, while Iraq recorded a modest recovery in output. Saudi Arabia's production fell by a further 240,000 barrels per day to 6.57 million barrels per day, leaving output down 37% from February levels. While extensive pipeline infrastructure has prevented a more dramatic decline - compared with production losses of around 80% in Kuwait and 70% in Iraq - it nevertheless highlights the kingdom's limited ability to offset regional supply disruptions while key export routes remain constrained.

Outside the Gulf, Venezuela continued to benefit from US involvement and its relative insulation from the crisis, with production rising to a fresh seven-year high of 1.18 million barrels per day.
Data from Bloomberg's monthly production survey.

bitcoin:native  Update — Bitcoin is sitting on the last major support zone before the chart starts to open up.

After the head and shoulders breakdown played out, price pushed straight into the 62k region. This level matters because it lines up with the 0.786 Fibonacci and the final major support area before my longer-term target around 52k.

That does not mean Bitcoin has to go straight to 52k.

But it does mean 62k is a massive level.

If Bitcoin can reclaim and hold above 62k to 63.7k, then bulls still have a chance to force a relief rally back toward 65k to 67k.

But if 62k is lost with strength, there really is not much clean structure between here and 52k. That’s where the downside picture starts getting a lot more serious.

Now layer in the macro because this sell-off wasn't just a crypto move.

The S&P 500 dropped 2.6%, wiping out around $1.8 trillion in market value. The Nasdaq fell 4.2%, semiconductors were smashed, and Asia followed with heavy selling across major markets.

The key driver was the jobs data.

The market wanted rate cuts. Instead, the May jobs report showed 172,000 jobs added, forcing traders to reprice the idea that the Fed may need to stay restrictive for longer. Goldman Sachs has now reportedly pushed its Fed rate-cut call into 2027, which shows how quickly sentiment shifted from “cuts are coming” to “higher for longer.”

That matters because when yields rise and liquidity expectations tighten, risk assets usually get hit first.

Crypto felt that straight away.

Bitcoin and alts got smashed with around $1.6B in crypto liquidations hitting the market during the move. Even gold sold off, which tells me this was not just fear. This was a full repricing of rates, liquidity, and inflation expectations.

Add in oil pushing higher as Middle East tensions intensify, and you can see why markets are under pressure.

In my opinion this is how I am reading the current outlook.

Bitcoin is sitting at a key technical level while the macro backdrop is risk-off.

Key levels I am watching:

• Current support: 62k
• Must reclaim: 62k to 63.7k
• Relief rally zone: 65k to 67k
• Major resistance: 70k to 75k
• Longer-term downside target: 52k

If Bitcoin can reclaim 62k, bulls can still force a bounce.

But if 62k fails properly, the 52k target becomes a much more realistic conversation.

Goldman Sachs just pushed its remaining Fed rate cuts into 2027.

But the real story is bigger than one forecast revision.

May payrolls added 172,000 jobs versus roughly 88,000 expected, while unemployment held at 4.3%. A resilient labor market gives the Fed little reason to rescue markets with easier policy.

Goldman now expects:

• No rate cuts in 2026
• Cuts in June and December 2027
• Core PCE above 3% throughout 2026
• A 20% probability of renewed rate hikes, up from 10%
• A 3.00–3.25% eventual terminal rate

The Fed’s current target range is still 3.50–3.75%.

Why this matters:
The market spent years asking when cuts would begin.
Now it may need to ask whether the next move is actually higher.

Strong employment + tariffs + elevated oil prices + persistent AI-related investment demand create an uncomfortable combination:

Growth remains resilient enough to prevent cuts, while inflation remains sticky enough to keep hikes on the table.

That is a tougher environment for:

• Long-duration growth stocks
• Highly leveraged companies
• Speculative assets dependent on cheap liquidity
• Businesses refinancing debt at higher rates

And potentially supportive for:

• Banks with improving net interest income
• Cash-rich, profitable companies
• The U.S. dollar
• Short-duration fixed income

Good economic news is no longer automatically good news for markets.

When valuations depend on falling rates, every strong jobs report becomes a liquidity warning.

At the start of the year, the consensus on Wall Street was close to unanimous. The dollar would weaken through 2026. Goldman Sachs put the dollar index in the low-90s, JPMorgan and MUFG landed in the same zone, and every one of those calls leaned on the same assumption: the Federal Reserve would cut rates and narrow the yield advantage that had supported the currency. The dollar declined about 11 percent in the first half of 2025, its steepest such drop since 1973, which made the bearish case look obvious. Then the war changed the inputs. The Hormuz oil shock pushed US headline inflation to 3.8 percent, the Fed lost its room to cut, and the dollar held near 99 instead of collapsing to the 90s. The most conssensual macro trade of the year became the one quietly running everybody over.

Don't get me wrong, the bearish case was not foolish. The dollar had just posted its worst first half in five decades, US growth was slowing, and a Fed easing cycle looked set to erode the rate gap that draws global capital into dollar assets. Strategists extrapolated that into a steady decline toward the low-90s and positioned for it. By the spring, the dollar was supposed to be well on its way down. Instead, the war rewrote the one variable the whole thesis depended on, which was the path of US interest rates.

The Hormuz premium did the damage to the forecast. Energy fed straight into US prices; headline CPI hit 3.8 percent in April, the highest reading since May 2023, and core PCE held at 3.3 percent. A central bank cannot ease into an inflation print that is reaccelerating on oil, so the cuts the dollar bears had penciled in for 2026 were postponed and then priced out entirely. The yield advantage that supports the dollar stayed wide. The currency held its ground while the consensus that bet against it slowly bled.

𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗽𝗽𝗲𝗱, 𝗡𝗼𝘁 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗼𝗻𝗴
The distinction that matters for the next move is what is actually holding the dollar up. This is not raw strength. The DXY sits below 100, which is weak against its own history, and per RBC, the currency is still roughly 15 percent ovvervalued against major peers on a purchasing-power basis. The dollar is being held in place by a single external force: an inflation rate that will not let the Fed cut. Remove that force, and the floor the bears were waiting for opens up.

I keep one rule about trades the whole Street agrees on. When every bank is sure the dollar drops, I want to know exactly what they are all leaning on, because the assumption everyone shares is the one nobody is hedging. This time, they were leaning on Fed cuts, and a war in the Gulf kicked the leg out.

That reframes what a dollar position actually is right now. A short dollar bet is a bet that the Fed cuts soon, which is a bet that inflation rolls over, which is a bet that the oil premium fades, which is a bet that the Hormuz war ends. Stripped down, the crowded dollar short is a leveraged wager on peace. Yesterday's session, where the war flared and the blockade stayed in place, is the kind of event that keeps that wager underwater. The dollar bears need a signed deal, and Washington spent the weekend making one harder to reach.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗾𝘂𝗲𝗲𝘇𝗲 𝗜𝘁 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝘀
A dollar that holds while oil sits near $96 is a tightening delivered to the rest of the world, whether they want it or not. Comodities are priced in dollars, so an importer pays twice during this war, once for the higher barrel and again for the firmer currency it must buy to settle the bill. The countries with dollar-denominated debt face a rising real burden on every coupon. Capital that might have rotated into emerging markets stays home for the yield. The pressure shows up as weaker local currencies, tighter financial conditions, and imported inflation across the importing bloc.

Energy importers have oil priced in dollars + a firm dollar = double cost. India imports 85% of its crude. Japan's yen near 158 makes the barrel brutal. Europe pays up on both.
Dollar debtors: EM sovereigns and corporates with dollar liabilities face a heavier real burden as the currency holds and local FX slips. Refinancing gets dearer.
EM capital flows: a wide US yield gap keeps capital in dollars. EM equity and local debt see outflows, central banks defend currencies, growth slows.

Now I will state the structural counterweight plainly.
The dollar is firm in the spot market and questioned in the reserve system at the same time. Underneath the cyclical bid, central banks keep trimming Treasury holdings and adding gold, a diversification that the asset freezes of Russia and now Iran have accelerated. The honest limit on the bullish-dollar view is that today's firmness and tomorrow's erosion are both real, on different clocks: the war props the spot price now, while the weaponization of reserves chips at the dollar's monetary role over years. A trader can be long the dollar this quarter and still believe its reserve share declines this decade.

The catalyst that ends the pain trade is specific, and it is not a chart pattern. A genuine Hormuz settlement lets the oil premium deflate, hands the Fed room to cut, and finally delivers the dollar decline the Street has been waiting for since January. The April ceasefire offered a preview when the dollar softened on the hope of de-escalation. Until that deal is signed, the inflation stays hot, the Fed stays pinned into Warsh's June 16-17 meeting, and the dollar keeps grinding the shorts. The position that pays is the patient one that stops fighting the prop.

Daily Update on Indian Market
1. The Macro Breakdown
The delicate quiet following Friday’s RBI policy pause has been completely shattered. Today, Monday, June 8, 2026, the Nifty 50 ran face-first into an institutional buzzsaw, collapsing 243.70 points (1.04%) to close at 23,123.00, while the Sensex plummeted 719 points. This is "The Kinetic Cascade." Over the weekend, the speculative "peace discount" built into global assets completely evaporated as a fresh, violent round of missile exchanges between Israel and Iran tore through local ceasefires. The immediate shockwave forced Brent Crude to surge back above $96 per barrel (spiking as high as $97.80 intraday) and sent the Indian Rupee tumbling 56 paise to close at a deeply stressed provisional low of 95.74. The post-policy safety net has ruptured; Dalal Street is realizing that no amount of emergency RBI capital-attracting measures can insulate an energy-dependent economy when regional conflicts actively reignite.
2. The Lagging Macro Reality
The Energy Friction Escalation: Brent Crude reclaiming the $94–$97 corridor instantly reactivates the worst-case fiscal deficit modeling for Dalal Street. Fitch Ratings dropped a stark note today predicting that crude will remain anchored at an elevated $100–$110 range through July due to persistent shipping bottlenecks, rendering the RBI's newly raised 5.1% inflation target highly conservative.
The Currency Bleed Reopens: The Rupee’s 56-paise plunge to 95.74 completely offsets the central bank’s recent structural adjustments. Despite relaxing Fully Accessible Route (FAR) parameters to lure foreign bonds, the broader global flight to safe-haven dollars completely overran the local currency desk today.
The IT Structural Crack: The defensive technology shield has completely fractured under the weight of active distribution. Wipro plummeted a devastating 8.45% to close at ₹181.60, and TCS dropped 2.25% to ₹2,149.40. Frontline IT is facing a massive, double-headed pincer: local corporate margin erosion paired with a relentless global asset re-allocation toward hardware-heavy, pure-play AI semiconductor hubs in East Asia.
3. Max Pain: The Nifty Magnet (June 11 Weekly Expiry)
Today's aggressive, high-volume liquidation has violently upended short-term derivatives open interest ahead of the June 11 weekly options expiration.

Nifty Bank managed a late, partial defend above 54,000, but unless the financials stage an emergency short-covering rally tomorrow morning, the technical gravity of the index will pull it straight toward the 23,000 absolute value pocket.
4. The "Predator Play" (Technical Strategy)
We follow the trend, but we never catch a falling knife without clear dynamic confirmation. The tactical operator treats clear moving average violations with clinical discipline.
Daily Chart Analysis: Nifty printed a wide-ranged, high-volume bearish marquee candle today that completely distances the index from its long-term 200-day SMA (~23,653) and its descending 20-day EMA (~23,710). Sectoral damage was broad-based, with Nifty Realty (-2.56%) and Nifty Metal (-2.33%) bearing the brunt of the capital flight.

Crucial Structural Support: 23,070 (Today's Intraday Panic Low Floor)
The Ultimate Liquidation Zone: 23,000 (The Core Psychological Value Base)

13/14 📈 FINANCE — Brent crude rose to ~$94.48/bbl (+1.5% today, +41% year-on-year) as Iran-Israel missile exchanges renewed supply fears; WTI settled near $91/bbl. Gold fell to a two-month low of $4,314/oz as Friday's blowout US jobs report (+172k vs 85k forecast) pushed 10-yr Treasury yields to 4.57% and lifted Fed rate-hike odds to ~70% by December. S&P 500 +0.30%, Nasdaq modestly higher, Dow -0.16%. #Markets #Oil #Gold #Finance #Stocks #Economy

3. 🚨 BREAKING: STRIKE EXCHANGE TRIGGERS GLOBAL MARKET SHOCK
DIRECT CONFRONTATION has reignited between Israel and Iran for the first time since the April 8 ceasefire. Israeli strikes hit a petrochemical complex at Mahshahr in southwest Iran, while Tehran retaliated with waves of ballistic missiles targeting central Israel. Brent crude has spiked over $97 per barrel as the fragile truce teeters on the edge of a total collapse.

Wind Financial Morning Post: June 9, 2026

Market Brief

Concerning data empowerment of AI, China has made its first systematic national-level deployment

Financial regulators urge strict crackdown on disorderly competition in financial sector

Trump urges de-escalation in Middle East; Iran and Israel halt mutual strikes

Asia-Pacific markets suffer “Black Monday,” with Korea’s KOSPI plunging over 8% and triggering two circuit breakers

U.S. chip stocks rebound sharply, Intel surges more than 11%

Apple unveils new AI platform and upgraded Siri, OS moves into “27 era”

NVIDIA expands AI compute partnership network, teams up with Korean firms to accelerate physical AI adoption

Global tech stocks plunge as Jensen Huang urges investors: “Be happy if you can buy at a discount”

Top Stories

Middle East tensions escalate briefly before de-escalation. At around midnight Beijing time on June 8, Iran launched another attack on Israel after a two-month hiatus; Israel retaliated immediately. U.S. President Trump urged both sides to halt hostilities swiftly and stated that sanctions on Iran would remain in place until an agreement is reached. After multiple rounds of strikes, Iran’s armed forces announced the conclusion of this round of military action against Israel, and Israel subsequently declared a temporary halt to its strikes on Iran. The Houthi movement in Yemen announced a ban on Israeli-linked vessels navigating the Red Sea.

NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang concluded a landmark visit to South Korea, securing major partnerships with SK Hynix, SK Telecom, Doosan Group, LG Group, and NAVER. Artificial intelligence and robotics are the focal areas of collaboration. In addition to co-building a data center with Hyundai Motor Group, NVIDIA’s collaborations with the other five firms involve AI factories. Its partnership with SK Hynix also includes joint R&D of next-generation AI memory chips. Regarding the recent tech stock sell-off, Huang stated investors should welcome the current market volatility, emphasizing that AI infrastructure is only at its beginning and urging investors to “buy the dip.”

Apple’s WWDC26 developer conference opened officially, marking the final WWDC under CEO Tim Cook’s tenure. No hardware was unveiled; all six operating systems—iOS 27, macOS 27, and others—were updated. The interface features Liquid Glass 2.0 with customizable global transparency. macOS has fully phased out Intel chips. The standout feature is the AI overhaul of Siri: it now operates as a standalone app, integrates with Google’s Gemini large model, and supports screen-aware, cross-app intelligent actions. Second-generation Apple Intelligence is fully embedded in native apps, backed by a privacy-focused hybrid cloud-edge architecture. The updates are now available to developers, with public beta testing in July and full rollout in autumn. Advanced AI features are limited to devices with A17 Pro or higher chips and are not yet available in mainland China.

China Macro & Policy

Chinese President Xi Jinping held talks with North Korean leader Kim Jong Un at the Mansudae Assembly Hall in Pyongyang. Xi outlined four proposals for advancing China-DPRK relations. He emphasized leveraging the full reopening of border checkpoints and the resumption of civil aviation and international passenger train services to expand people-to-people exchanges and achieve mutual engagement.

The National Financial Regulatory Administration has outlined key priorities, stressing the need to guard against external shocks and continuously refine contingency plans. It reaffirmed its commitment to strict and rigorous regulation: tightening entry thresholds, enforcing rules decisively, and punishing violations severely to ensure “teeth and thorns” in oversight. Accountability will be rigorously enforced to ensure responsibility is fulfilled. The administration is firmly advancing the reduction and quality improvement of small and medium-sized financial institutions and intensifying efforts to rectify disorderly competition in the financial sector.

The European Union is advancing legislation aimed at forcing companies to avoid overreliance on single suppliers as part of its effort to reset trade relations with China. China’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs spokesperson Lin Jian responded that China is open and proactive in resolving Sino-European trade disputes and hopes the EU will work with China through dialogue and consultation to address differences.

China Equities Markets

A-shares opened sharply lower and traded weakly throughout the session. The SSE Index fell 1.7% to 3,959.34, the Shenzhen Component Index dropped 3.22%, the ChiNext Index slid 3.69%, the Star 50 Index declined 4.3%, and the Wind All China Index fell 2.76%. Trading volume reached 2.82 trillion yuan. Semiconductor, AI infrastructure, and CPO-related stocks tumbled, while the nonferrous metals sector weakened. Robotics, physical AI, and oil & gas stocks outperformed.

Hong Kong markets declined amid volatility. The Hang Seng Index fell 1.22% to 24,657.06, the Hang Seng Tech Index dropped 2.71%, and the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index slid 1.13%. AI tech stocks fell broadly; nonferrous metals, electrical equipment, and semiconductors led losses. Oil and coal stocks rose. Net inflows via the Southbound Stock Connect totaled 11.32 billion yuan.

Taiwan’s Weighted Index closed down 3.48% at 43,502.78, marking its third consecutive sharp decline. TSMC fell nearly 3%, Hon Hai Precision fell over 5%, and MediaTek dropped more than 5%.

Following ChangXin Memory and Unitree Robotics, another hard-tech firm is set to appear before the review committee. Exchange records show that SuYuan Technology’s STAR Market IPO application and YueXin Semiconductor’s ChiNext IPO application will be reviewed on June 15. SuYuan, one of China’s “Big Four” domestic GPU startups, aims to raise approximately 6 billion yuan. YueXin Semiconductor plans to raise 7.5 billion yuan.

China Industry News

The National Data Bureau released the “Implementation Plan for Promoting High-Quality Industry-Specific Datasets.” This marks the first systematic national-level deployment to empower AI development through data. The plan aims to advance the construction of high-quality multimodal datasets—including text, images, and audiovisual content—aligned with AI application needs. Priority sectors include scientific research, industrial manufacturing, low-altitude economy, and embodied intelligence.

The first major national landmark project to commence during China’s 15th Five-Year Plan period—the Three Gorges Waterway New Channel—officially broke ground. The project includes a new navigation channel for the Three Gorges Dam and an expansion of the Gezhouba Ship Lock, designed for 10,000-ton vessels, with a total investment of approximately 77.2 billion yuan.

The State Council issued the “15th Five-Year Plan for Modern Emergency Management System,” outlining priorities for workplace safety, disaster prevention, and mitigation through 2030. The plan includes 17 key projects across five categories, aiming to establish a prevention-oriented governance model by 2030.

The National Health Commission and 13 other departments released key tasks for 2026 to rectify malpractice in pharmaceutical procurement and medical services. The measures include cracking down on price violations such as failure to display prices, price fraud, and collusion to manipulate drug prices. Unregulated “quick-certification” cosmetic medical training programs will also be targeted.

In response to issues such as exaggerated claims, “review-only, no testing,” and commercialized product evaluations, the National Internet Information Office and the State Administration for Market Regulation jointly issued the “Regulations on Network Evaluation Activities,” requiring that any evaluation involving product functionality or performance must be conducted by a legally accredited inspection and testing institution.

According to the China Passenger Car Association, retail sales of passenger vehicles in May totaled 1.51 million units, down 22.1% year-over-year but up 9.2% month-over-month. Retail sales of new energy vehicles reached 950,000 units, down 7.5% year-over-year but up 12.4% month-over-month, with NEV penetration reaching 62.9% of total passenger vehicle retail sales.

The revised “Good Clinical Practice for Drug Clinical Trials” will take effect on September 1. The revision emphasizes that participant safety and data reliability are non-negotiable. It encourages the standardized application of AI and other new technologies.

Guangzhou has introduced new housing fund loan rules: adding loans for construction, renovation, and major repairs; relaxing eligibility for foreigners; increasing loan calculation coefficients; and optimizing loan terms. The rules also introduce new disqualifying scenarios to prevent risks such as illiquid properties, fraudulent transactions, and divorce-driven loan abuse.

Hong Kong’s government announced that the “Guangdong Vehicles to Hong Kong” program will expand to cover all of Guangdong Province. Daily reservation quotas for entering urban areas will increase from 100 to 200 vehicles per day (with a maximum three-day stay per vehicle), and a new “EasyPark & Explore” parking service at border checkpoints will be launched.

Fintech

Sources say Visa and Mastercard are in discussions with Stripe, Coinbase, and others to form a stablecoin consortium and launch a new stablecoin platform. Talks may involve additional participants. If finalized, the initiative could significantly reshape the $300 billion stablecoin market.

Corporate News & Updates

Multiple sources indicate that major AI chip design firms have quietly begun considering Intel as an alternative foundry partner. Google has formally placed an order with Intel to produce over 3 million TPU chips by 2028. NVIDIA is testing Intel’s 18A manufacturing process to evaluate its suitability for next-generation GPU architectures. Meanwhile, SK Hynix is assessing compatibility between its memory chips and Intel’s packaging technology.

Alibaba announced the merger of its Tongyi large model division and Future Life Lab into a new unit called Token Foundry, directly overseen by CEO Wu Yongming. Zhou Jingren will serve as Alibaba’s chief scientist and lead the establishment of Alibaba’s AI Future Research Institute, focused on frontier AI breakthroughs. Zheng Bo and his teams, including Happy Horse and Happy Oyster, will join Token Foundry.

https://t.co/zDq3KwGBbd and Tencent will deepen collaboration on AI agents. Leveraging JD’s supply chain and fulfillment capabilities and Tencent’s ecosystem access, the two firms aim to co-develop a new paradigm of cross-scenario intelligent services, advancing AI agents from isolated applications to ecosystem-wide coordination.

WeChat is conducting a limited beta test of a new search function for Moments on select iOS and Android users. Users can now tap a magnifying glass icon on the “Me > Moments” page to search historical Moments content and filter by friend or time. WeChat also announced it will open APIs to developers for integration with its AI ecosystem.

Tesla chief designer Franz von Holzhausen recently revealed that the new-generation Roadster electric sports car is in final countdown, with an unveiling expected within weeks.

Amazon announced a multi-year, multi-billion-dollar agreement with Corning to procure fiber optic products for its U.S. data centers. Corning will supply fiber, cabling, and connectivity solutions to support Amazon’s expanding U.S. infrastructure.

Moonshot AI is launching a new funding round aiming to raise up to $2 billion, which would value the startup at $30 billion—nearly seven times its December 2025 valuation of $4.3 billion—and surpass its Hong Kong-listed rival MiniMax.

Global Macro & Policy

On June 8, U.S. forces fired on a fuel tanker in the Gulf of Oman that was attempting to reach an Iranian port, disabling its navigation. Since April 13, the U.S. has disabled seven merchant vessels and forced 134 others to alter course as part of its maritime blockade against Iran. The U.S. State Department stated the blockade will remain in place until an agreement is reached, and frozen Iranian assets will not be released until Iran fulfills its commitments.

The New York Fed’s latest survey showed Americans’ views on the labor market turned pessimistic in May, with job seekers reporting the weakest employment outlook since the start of the year. Inflation expectations remained broadly unchanged from the prior month.

Bank of England policymaker Alan Taylor explicitly stated that the current 3.75% interest rate is already restrictive and that no further rate hikes are needed to counter inflation pressures stemming from the Iran conflict. Taylor, the committee’s most vocal advocate for rate cuts, has repeatedly called for policy easing before the conflict.

IMF Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva warned that the global economy is entering an era of “persistent shocks,” urging governments, international institutions, and corporations to accept that future crises will be more frequent and more interconnected, requiring stronger systemic resilience.

Germany’s factory orders in April fell 3.8% month-over-month, worse than the 2% expected decline, intensifying concerns that the economy may contract in the second quarter amid the impact of Middle East conflict and soaring energy costs.

An Indian trade official said India is pushing the U.S. for more favorable tariff treatment as part of negotiations for a provisional trade agreement. India is also seeking assurances that the U.S. will not impose additional tariffs on India after any deal is finalized.

Global Equity Markets

U.S. major indices closed mixed. The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 0.16% to 50,786.01, the S&P 500 rose 0.3% to 7,405.73, and the Nasdaq Composite gained 0.86% to 25,929.66. Travelers Companies dropped over 2%, and Apple fell nearly 2%, leading losses in the Dow. Chip stocks rebounded: the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index surged 5.61%, Intel rose over 11%, and Marvell Technology climbed more than 9%. The Wind US Magnificent Seven Index fell 0.22%, with Google and Microsoft each down over 1%. The Nasdaq China Golden Dragon Index declined 0.62%, with Lufax falling over 5% and Daqo New Energy dropping more than 4%.

European major indices closed mixed. Germany’s DAX fell 0.58% to 24,616.22, France’s CAC 40 dropped 0.23% to 8,199.29, and the UK’s FTSE 100 edged up 0.05% to 10,373.20. AI stocks continued to sell off, while rising expectations of ECB rate hikes weighed on broader equities.

Asia-Pacific markets suffered “Black Monday.” South Korea’s KOSPI plunged 8.29% to 7,484.41, triggering two circuit breakers; the KOSDAQ fell over 9%. Samsung Electronics dropped more than 10%, and SK Hynix slid nearly 8%. Japan’s Nikkei 225 fell 3.85% to 64,024.60; SoftBank Group dropped 6%, and Kioxia fell 8%. India’s S&P BSE SENSEX fell 0.97% to 73,524.26.

Sources say SpaceX’s IPO subscription demand has far exceeded expectations, signaling strong investor appetite for what could become the largest tech IPO in history. Underwriters are expected to close institutional book-building after Wednesday’s New York close.

OpenAI has secretly submitted a draft S-1 registration statement to the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission but has not yet decided on an IPO timeline.

Bonds

China’s interbank bond market saw renewed adjustment, with yields on major government bonds rising. One-year Treasuries held relatively firm. Treasury futures declined across the board, with the 30-year contract down 0.25%. Market liquidity tightened; the People’s Bank of China conducted 218.5 billion yuan in reverse repurchase operations, net injecting 207.5 billion yuan.

Amazon plans to issue up to 14 billion Canadian dollars (approximately $10 billion) in investment-grade Canadian dollar-denominated bonds, which would set a new record for corporate bond issuance in Canadian currency.

U.S. Treasury yields rose across the curve: 2-year yield up 2.31 bps to 4.162%, 3-year yield up 2.61 bps to 4.215%, 5-year yield up 2.84 bps to 4.290%, 10-year yield up 4.18 bps to 4.562%, and 30-year yield up 4.36 bps to 5.036%.

Commodities

Precious metals futures ended mostly lower. COMEX gold futures fell 0.26% to $4,353.80 per ounce; COMEX silver futures dropped 1.13% to $68.33 per ounce. Rising energy prices and inflation concerns, coupled with stronger-than-expected U.S. employment data reinforcing Fed rate hike expectations, pressured non-yielding assets and triggered capital outflows.

U.S. crude oil futures rose 0.82% to $91.28 per barrel; Brent crude futures gained 1.10% to $94.11 per barrel. Although fears of supply disruption eased after Iran and Israel signaled avoidance of further escalation, global crude inventories have fallen below the 100-day warning line, significantly reducing market buffers. Transport restrictions through the Strait of Hormuz persist, and OPEC+’s modest output increase is insufficient to offset supply tightness, keeping geopolitical risk premiums elevated.

London metal prices were mixed. LME copper rose 0.53% to $13,590.50 per ton, aluminum climbed 0.43% to $3,607.50 per ton, zinc gained 0.13% to $3,534.50 per ton, lead fell 0.82% to $1,988.50 per ton, nickel dropped 0.97% to $18,400.00 per ton, and tin declined 1.58% to $52,100.00 per ton.

FX & Currency Markets

On Monday, the onshore renminbi closed at 6.7844 per U.S. dollar, down 132 basis points from the previous close. The offshore rate closed at 6.7842. The PBOC’s central parity rate was set at 6.8198, a 41-basis-point depreciation from the prior day.

At New York’s close, the U.S. dollar index fell 0.06% to 100.01. Most non-U.S. currencies rose: EUR/USD gained 0.09% to 1.1532, GBP/USD edged down 0.01% to 1.3338, AUD/USD rose 0.02% to 0.7045, USD/JPY fell 0.09% to 160.1765, USD/CAD rose 0.08% to 1.3954, USD/CHF climbed 0.19% to 0.7980, and the offshore renminbi strengthened 64 bps to 6.7848.

The Korean won hit its lowest level against the dollar since the financial crisis. President Yoon Suk Yeol said the current foreign exchange situation appears temporary rather than systemic and emphasized that Korean equities remain undervalued. Authorities met with banks in the afternoon to discuss forex issues and pledged strict action against any market manipulation.

Indonesia’s foreign exchange reserves fell for the fifth consecutive month, reaching $144.9 billion—the longest continuous decline since 2018. The central bank cited debt repayments and efforts to stabilize the rupiah as causes. The rupiah has depreciated about 8% year-to-date.
